title: Auschwitz - Tätergeständnisse und Augenzeugen des Holocaust
description: Auschwitz - Tätergeständnisse und Augenzeugen des Holocaust is a historical revisionism / holocaust denial by Jürgen Graf. Confiscated by the Mannheim Regional Court in November 1994.
descriptionParagraphs: Auschwitz - Tätergeständnisse und Augenzeugen des Holocaust is a historical revisionism / holocaust denial by Jürgen Graf. Confiscated by the Mannheim Regional Court in November 1994.

The surviving record is interesting because it shows how even ordinary-looking books can acquire a charged political afterlife. As a historical revisionism / holocaust denial, it can be read not only for subject matter but for the way form, tone, and circulation make a text feel dangerous, intimate, or politically usable to anxious officials.

It also matters as part of a wider censorship history in List of books confiscated for violating Criminal Code 86, 86a, 130 or 130a. The present page is a dossier starter built from source-tracked ban records; the surviving note currently says: Confiscated by the Mannheim Regional Court in November 1994. Unofficial title translation: Auschwitz - Confessions of Perpetrators and Eyewitnesses of the Holocaust More publication history, translations, and close reading can be added later.

banningParagraphs: Auschwitz - Tätergeständnisse und Augenzeugen des Holocaust entered censorship debates as a historical revisionism / holocaust denial associated with controversy, publication history, and state scrutiny. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around political sensitivity.

The earliest event currently captured here is Date not yet pinned down in Germany, where List of books confiscated for violating Criminal Code 86, 86a, 130 or 130a authorities banned publication or circulation. counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.
